messages.content: What should I check before pushing a PR?

Before pushing a PR, make sure your branch is rebased on the latest main, all tests pass locally, lint and type checks are clean, and your commit messages clearly describe the change. Also self-review the diff one more time to catch leftover debug logs or commented-out code.
How do I write a good PR description?

A good PR description explains the why, not just the what. Start with a one-line summary, then describe the problem, the approach you took, and any trade-offs. Include a test plan with steps reviewers can follow, and link related issues or tickets.
What makes a code review helpful?

A helpful code review is specific, kind, and focused on the change at hand. Call out concrete issues with line references, suggest alternatives rather than just rejecting, separate blocking comments from nits, and approve quickly when the change is solid so the author isn't blocked.
How should I respond to review comments?

Treat review comments as collaboration, not criticism. Reply to each thread, either with the fix commit, a question, or a respectful disagreement backed by reasoning. Resolve threads only after the reviewer is satisfied, and thank reviewers for catches that improved the change.
When should I split a PR into smaller ones?

Split a PR when it touches unrelated concerns, exceeds a few hundred lines of meaningful diff, or mixes refactors with behavior changes. Smaller PRs get reviewed faster, are easier to revert, and reduce the chance that a single bug blocks the whole change.
What's a good commit message style?

Use a short imperative subject under 72 characters (e.g. 'fix: handle empty cart on checkout'), then a blank line, then a body that explains why the change is needed and any non-obvious details. Reference issues at the bottom. Keep one logical change per commit.
How do I avoid merge conflicts?

Keep branches short-lived, rebase on main frequently, and pull before you start work each day. Coordinate with teammates on shared files, and avoid wide formatting-only changes mixed with logic edits. When conflicts do happen, resolve them locally and re-run tests before pushing.
Should I review my own PR before requesting reviewers?

Yes, always self-review first. Open the diff in the PR view, read it as if you were a stranger, and leave inline comments explaining tricky parts. You'll catch typos, dead code, and missing tests, and reviewers will spend their time on substance instead of basic cleanup.
